nodes.label: all concepts
nodes.description: Every term from the three decks connected by course, section, semantic role, phrase pattern, and lexical similarity.

nodes.label: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.description: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class

nodes.label: who or what counts
nodes.description: Semantic hub: who or what counts

nodes.label: voice body and presentation
nodes.description: Semantic hub: voice body and presentation

nodes.label: power burden and harm
nodes.description: Semantic hub: power burden and harm

nodes.label: laws institutions and events
nodes.description: Semantic hub: laws institutions and events

nodes.label: value = what receives weight
nodes.description: Phrase pattern: value = what receives weight

nodes.label: Environmental ethics
nodes.definition: The branch of philosophy that extends moral consideration beyond humans to the non-human world — animals, plants, ecosystems, the biosphere. It studies the moral relationship between humanity and nature.

nodes.label: Ethics
nodes.definition: The systematic study of right and wrong, good and bad — the moral principles that guide conduct. Environmental ethics asks how those principles apply to the natural world.

nodes.label: Anthropocentrism
nodes.definition: "Human-centered." The view that only humans have intrinsic moral worth; nature matters only as a resource for human benefit. The dominant Western worldview.

nodes.label: Biocentrism
nodes.definition: "Life-centered." The view that all living things have inherent worth and deserve moral consideration, not just humans. Linked to Paul Taylor and Albert Schweitzer.

nodes.label: Ecocentrism
nodes.definition: "Ecosystem-centered." The view that whole ecological wholes — species, ecosystems, the biosphere — have intrinsic value, not just individual organisms. Rooted in Leopold's land ethic.

nodes.label: Deep ecology
nodes.definition: A holistic, ecocentric movement (coined by Arne Naess, 1973) holding that nature has intrinsic value independent of human use, requiring a radical rethinking of the human–nature relationship and the self.

nodes.label: Shallow ecology
nodes.definition: Naess's term for mainstream environmentalism that fights pollution and resource depletion mainly to protect human health and prosperity — i.e., it stays anthropocentric. Contrast: deep ecology.

nodes.label: Biocentric egalitarianism
nodes.definition: The deep-ecology principle that all living things have equal intrinsic value and an equal right to live and flourish.

nodes.label: Gaia hypothesis
nodes.definition: James Lovelock's theory that Earth functions as a single self-regulating living system, in which life and its physical environment interact to keep conditions suitable for life.

nodes.label: Speciesism
nodes.definition: Discrimination against other species based on assumed human superiority — treating one's own species' interests as more important simply because of species membership. Popularized by Peter Singer (term coined by Richard Ryder).

nodes.label: Ethical egoism
nodes.definition: The theory that one ought to act in one's own self-interest. Applied to the environment, it justifies protecting nature only when doing so benefits oneself.

nodes.label: Utilitarianism
nodes.definition: The right act produces the greatest good for the greatest number. Because animals feel pleasure and pain, utilitarianism can extend moral consideration to them. (Bentham, Mill, Singer.)

nodes.label: Moral standing (considerability)
nodes.definition: The status of being an entity whose interests matter morally and must be taken into account. Environmental ethics debates which entities — animals, plants, ecosystems — have it.

nodes.label: Sentientism
nodes.definition: The view that the capacity to feel pain and pleasure (sentience) is the criterion for moral consideration: a being deserves concern if it can suffer.

nodes.label: Holism (moral)
nodes.definition: The view that moral value belongs to whole systems — ecosystems, species, the biotic community — rather than only to individuals. Characteristic of ecocentrism and the land ethic.

nodes.label: Individualism (moral)
nodes.definition: The view that only individual organisms — not collectives like species or ecosystems — can bear moral value. Characteristic of animal-rights and biocentric individualism.

nodes.label: Intrinsic vs. instrumental value
nodes.definition: Intrinsic: worth in and of itself. Instrumental (extrinsic): worth only as a means/useful to others. The core axis of the field — does nature have value beyond its use to humans?

nodes.label: Intrinsic value
nodes.definition: Value something has in itself, for its own sake, independent of usefulness to anyone. Non-anthropocentric ethics holds that nature possesses it.

nodes.label: Instrumental (extrinsic) value
nodes.definition: Value something has as a means to an end — its usefulness to others. Anthropocentrism treats nature as having only instrumental value to humans.

nodes.label: Conservation
nodes.definition: The wise, sustainable use of natural resources for long-term human benefit (utilitarian). Gifford Pinchot: "the greatest good for the greatest number for the longest time."

nodes.label: Preservation
nodes.definition: Protecting nature in its pristine, untouched state for its own sake and for spiritual/aesthetic value. Championed by John Muir.

nodes.label: Conservation vs. preservation
nodes.definition: The founding debate: Pinchot's utilitarian conservation (use resources wisely for people) vs. Muir's biocentric preservation (protect wild nature for its intrinsic worth).

nodes.label: Non-anthropocentrism
nodes.definition: Any approach granting moral consideration to non-humans — biocentrism, ecocentrism, deep ecology. The opposite of anthropocentrism.

nodes.label: Sustainability
nodes.definition: Meeting present needs without degrading the environment or depleting resources, so ecological and human systems can endure indefinitely.

nodes.label: Sustainable development
nodes.definition: "Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs." — Brundtland Report, Our Common Future (1987).

nodes.label: Three pillars of sustainability
nodes.definition: The integration of environment, economy, and social equity — sometimes "planet, profit, people" or the triple bottom line.

nodes.label: Traditional Ecological Knowledge (TEK)
nodes.definition: Knowledge, practices, and beliefs about living things and their environment, built up by Indigenous and local peoples over generations and passed down culturally.

nodes.label: Lynn White thesis
nodes.definition: In "The Historical Roots of Our Ecologic Crisis" (1967), Lynn White Jr. argued that Judeo-Christian belief in human dominion over nature is a root cause of the Western environmental crisis. He named St. Francis the patron saint of ecology.

nodes.label: Dominion (Judeo-Christian)
nodes.definition: The biblical idea (Genesis 1:28) that God gave humans dominion over Earth and its creatures. Criticized as license for exploitation — but reinterpreted by others as a duty of care.

nodes.label: Stewardship
nodes.definition: The view that humans are caretakers/trustees of creation, responsible for protecting it rather than exploiting it — a reinterpretation of "dominion."

nodes.label: Eastern religious views
nodes.definition: Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Taoism emphasize interconnectedness, non-harm, and harmony with nature rather than human domination over it.

nodes.label: Ahimsa
nodes.definition: The principle of non-violence and non-harm to all living beings — central to Jainism, Buddhism, and Hinduism; grounds respect for all life.

nodes.label: Khalifa (Islamic stewardship)
nodes.definition: In Islam, the idea that humans are God's trustees (khalifa) of the Earth, accountable for its care.

nodes.label: Ecofeminism
nodes.definition: Links the domination of nature to the domination of women, arguing both stem from patriarchal hierarchies. Associated with Karen Warren and Val Plumwood.

nodes.label: Cornucopian view
nodes.definition: The optimistic belief that human ingenuity and technology will overcome resource limits — resources are effectively unlimited. Associated with Julian Simon.

nodes.label: Neo-Malthusianism
nodes.definition: The view (after Malthus) that unchecked population growth outstrips resources and ends in catastrophe; supports population control. Associated with Paul Ehrlich, The Population Bomb (1968).

nodes.label: Animal liberation
nodes.definition: Peter Singer's utilitarian position (Animal Liberation, 1975): because animals suffer, their interests must be weighed equally; ignoring this is speciesism.

nodes.label: Animal rights
nodes.definition: Tom Regan's deontological position (The Case for Animal Rights, 1983): animals are "subjects-of-a-life" with inherent value and rights that may not be violated, whatever the consequences.

nodes.label: Subjects-of-a-life
nodes.definition: Tom Regan's criterion: beings with beliefs, desires, perception, memory, and a sense of their own future have inherent value — and therefore rights.

nodes.label: The Land Ethic
nodes.definition: Aldo Leopold's principle (1949): humans are plain members of the biotic community, not its conquerors. "A thing is right when it tends to preserve the integrity, stability, and beauty of the biotic community."

nodes.label: Reverence for life
nodes.definition: Albert Schweitzer's ethic that all life is sacred and deserves respect — an early biocentric idea.

nodes.label: Respect for Nature
nodes.definition: Paul Taylor's biocentric theory (1986): every organism is a "teleological center of life" pursuing its own good, so each has inherent worth and deserves moral respect.

nodes.label: Transcendentalist view of nature
nodes.definition: The 19th-century idea (Emerson, Thoreau) that nature is a source of spiritual truth, beauty, and moral renewal — value beyond material use.

nodes.label: John Muir
nodes.definition: (1838–1914) "Father of the National Parks." Founded the Sierra Club (1892); champion of preservationism; fought (and lost) the Hetch Hetchy damming; saw wilderness as sacred.

nodes.label: Gifford Pinchot
nodes.definition: (1865–1946) First Chief of the U.S. Forest Service (1905); founder of American conservationism / "wise use." Rival of Muir over Hetch Hetchy.

nodes.label: Aldo Leopold
nodes.definition: (1887–1948) Wrote A Sand County Almanac (1949); originated the Land Ethic and "thinking like a mountain"; founder of wildlife ecology.

nodes.label: Garrett Hardin
nodes.definition: (1915–2003) Ecologist who wrote "The Tragedy of the Commons" (1968) and "lifeboat ethics" — shared resources get overexploited by self-interested individuals.

nodes.label: Rachel Carson
nodes.definition: (1907–1964) Marine biologist whose Silent Spring (1962) exposed the dangers of pesticides (DDT); launched the modern environmental movement and led to DDT's ban.

nodes.label: Arne Naess
nodes.definition: (1912–2009) Norwegian philosopher who coined "deep ecology" (1973), distinguished it from "shallow" environmentalism, and argued for biocentric egalitarianism.

nodes.label: James Lovelock
nodes.definition: (1919–2022) British scientist who proposed the Gaia hypothesis — Earth as a single self-regulating living system.

nodes.label: Henry David Thoreau
nodes.definition: (1817–1862) Transcendentalist author of Walden (1854); celebrated simple living in nature and is a forerunner of environmental thought.

nodes.label: Ralph Waldo Emerson
nodes.definition: (1803–1882) Transcendentalist; his essay Nature (1836) framed the natural world as a source of spiritual insight and unity.

nodes.label: Lynn White Jr.
nodes.definition: (1907–1987) Historian whose 1967 essay blamed Judeo-Christian anthropocentrism for environmental degradation.

nodes.label: Peter Singer
nodes.definition: (b. 1946) Utilitarian philosopher; Animal Liberation (1975) argued that ignoring animal suffering is speciesism.

nodes.label: Tom Regan
nodes.definition: (1938–2017) Philosopher; The Case for Animal Rights (1983) argued animals are "subjects-of-a-life" with inherent value and rights.

nodes.label: Holmes Rolston III
nodes.definition: (b. 1932) Called the "father of environmental ethics" as a discipline; argued nature has objective intrinsic value humans must respect.

nodes.label: Christopher Stone
nodes.definition: (1937–2021) Legal scholar; "Should Trees Have Standing?" (1972) argued natural objects deserve legal rights and standing to sue.

nodes.label: Paul Taylor
nodes.definition: (1923–2015) Philosopher; Respect for Nature (1986) developed biocentric egalitarianism — each organism a "teleological center of life."

nodes.label: John Evelyn
nodes.definition: (1620–1706) English writer whose treatise Fumifugium (1661) condemned London's coal-smoke air pollution — an early warning about industrialization.

nodes.label: Theodore Roosevelt
nodes.definition: (1858–1919) U.S. President who vastly expanded national parks, forests, and wildlife refuges; allied with Pinchot on conservation.

nodes.label: Paul Ehrlich
nodes.definition: (b. 1932) Biologist; The Population Bomb (1968) warned of mass starvation from overpopulation (neo-Malthusian).

nodes.label: Albert Schweitzer
nodes.definition: (1875–1965) Theologian-physician who articulated "reverence for life" — an early biocentric ethic.

nodes.label: Silent Spring (1962)
nodes.definition: Rachel Carson's landmark book documenting the ecological damage of pesticides like DDT. Ignited the modern environmental movement; opposed chiefly by chemical companies.

nodes.label: The Tragedy of the Commons (1968)
nodes.definition: Garrett Hardin's essay: individuals exploiting a shared resource for personal gain deplete it, harming all. Argues for regulation or shared restraint.

nodes.label: A Sand County Almanac (1949)
nodes.definition: Aldo Leopold's book introducing the Land Ethic — one of the most influential works in environmental philosophy. Promotes the idea of a "land ethic."

nodes.label: Earth Day (April 22, 1970)
nodes.definition: The first nationwide U.S. environmental demonstration; marked the birth of modern environmental consciousness and helped spur landmark legislation.

nodes.label: Hetch Hetchy controversy
nodes.definition: The 1908–1913 fight over damming Yosemite's Hetch Hetchy Valley to supply San Francisco's water. Pitted Muir (preservation) vs. Pinchot (conservation); the valley was flooded — a defining moment for both movements.

nodes.label: Cuyahoga River fire (1969)
nodes.definition: Cleveland's Cuyahoga River caught fire from industrial pollution — a powerful symbol that prompted the clean-water initiatives of the 1970s.

nodes.label: Sierra Club (1892)
nodes.definition: One of the first and most influential environmental organizations, founded by John Muir to protect wilderness — especially the Sierra Nevada.

nodes.label: U.S. Forest Service (1905)
nodes.definition: Federal agency created to manage national forests for sustainable use; first led by Gifford Pinchot.

nodes.label: Love Canal (1978)
nodes.definition: A Niagara Falls, NY neighborhood built on a buried toxic-waste dump; residents' health crisis led directly to the creation of CERCLA / Superfund (1980).

nodes.label: Bhopal disaster (1984)
nodes.definition: A toxic gas leak at a Union Carbide pesticide plant in Bhopal, India killed thousands — the worst industrial disaster in history and a landmark environmental-justice case.

nodes.label: Exxon Valdez (1989)
nodes.definition: A tanker spilled millions of gallons of crude oil into Prince William Sound, Alaska; led to the Oil Pollution Act of 1990.

nodes.label: Dust Bowl (1930s)
nodes.definition: Drought plus poor farming caused massive dust storms across the U.S. Great Plains; spurred national soil-conservation efforts.

nodes.label: Environmental justice
nodes.definition: The fair treatment and meaningful involvement of all people — regardless of race or income — in environmental policy, so no group bears a disproportionate share of environmental harms.

nodes.label: Environmental racism
nodes.definition: The disproportionate siting of pollution and toxic waste in communities of color, plus their exclusion from environmental decisions. Term coined by Benjamin Chavis (1982).

nodes.label: Environmental injustice
nodes.definition: The unfair loading of environmental burdens — pollution, hazards — onto poor, marginalized, or minority communities that often lack power to resist.

nodes.label: Warren County, NC (1982)
nodes.definition: Protest against a PCB-contaminated soil landfill placed in a majority-Black county; 500+ arrested. Widely regarded as the birthplace of the environmental justice movement.

nodes.label: Benjamin Chavis
nodes.definition: Civil-rights leader who coined "environmental racism" from a jail cell during the 1982 Warren County protests; later directed the UCC Commission for Racial Justice.

nodes.label: Robert Bullard
nodes.definition: Sociologist known as the "Father of Environmental Justice"; author of Dumping in Dixie (1990), the first major study documenting environmental racism.

nodes.label: Toxic Wastes and Race (1987)
nodes.definition: The landmark UCC Commission for Racial Justice report that statistically documented the nationwide correlation between toxic-waste sites and minority communities.

nodes.label: Executive Order 12898 (1994)
nodes.definition: Signed by President Clinton; directed federal agencies to identify and address disproportionately high environmental and health effects on minority and low-income populations.

nodes.label: Principles of Environmental Justice (1991)
nodes.definition: The 17 founding principles adopted at the First National People of Color Environmental Leadership Summit, defining the movement's goals.

nodes.label: Toxic tort
nodes.definition: A legal claim for harm caused by exposure to a toxic substance (e.g., a chemical above "maximum contaminant levels" in drinking water). The mechanism behind much environmental-rights litigation.

nodes.label: NIMBY ("Not In My Back Yard")
nodes.definition: Residents opposing undesirable facilities — landfills, incinerators, plants — near their homes. Such facilities are often pushed instead onto poorer communities, creating injustice.

nodes.label: Intergenerational justice
nodes.definition: The principle that environmental justice is intergenerational — each generation has a duty to preserve the environment and resources for future generations.

nodes.label: Distributive justice
nodes.definition: Fairness in how environmental benefits and burdens (pollution, clean-resource access) are distributed across society.

nodes.label: Procedural justice
nodes.definition: Fairness in the decision-making process — ensuring affected communities have a meaningful voice in environmental decisions.

nodes.label: Climate justice
nodes.definition: The view that climate change disproportionately harms the poor and developing nations (who contributed least), and that responses must address this inequity.

nodes.label: Flint water crisis (2014)
nodes.definition: Lead contamination of drinking water in majority-Black, low-income Flint, Michigan — a prominent recent case of environmental injustice.

nodes.label: Cancer Alley
nodes.definition: An 85-mile stretch of the Mississippi in Louisiana lined with petrochemical plants, where predominantly Black communities suffer elevated cancer rates. A symbol of environmental racism.

nodes.label: Human rights & environmental justice
nodes.definition: The movement frames a clean, healthy environment as a basic human right and draws directly on the strategies and moral framework of the civil-rights movement.

nodes.label: Industrial / corporate interests
nodes.definition: Polluting industries tend to place hazardous facilities where land is cheap and political resistance weakest — disproportionately poor and minority areas — putting profit over community health.

nodes.label: Environmental effects on health
nodes.definition: EJ centers on health: communities near pollution sources show higher rates of asthma, cancer, and other illnesses — the human cost that drives the movement's community focus.

nodes.label: NEPA (1969/1970)
nodes.definition: National Environmental Policy Act — the foundational U.S. environmental law. Requires federal agencies to file an Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) and to weigh environmental consequences. Promotes informed federal decision-making.

nodes.label: Environmental Impact Statement (EIS)
nodes.definition: A detailed report required by NEPA analyzing a proposed federal action's environmental effects and alternatives — the most visible NEPA requirement.

nodes.label: EPA (1970)
nodes.definition: Environmental Protection Agency — created by President Nixon to consolidate federal environmental regulation, research, and enforcement of pollution-control laws.

nodes.label: Clean Air Act (1970)
nodes.definition: The major U.S. air-pollution law; lets EPA set National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS). Strengthened by 1977 and 1990 amendments.

nodes.label: Clean Water Act (1972)
nodes.definition: Originally the Federal Water Pollution Control Act Amendments. Regulates pollutant discharge into navigable waters, sets water-quality standards, and protects wetlands.

nodes.label: Safe Drinking Water Act (1974)
nodes.definition: Sets national standards for contaminants ("maximum contaminant levels") in public drinking water and protects underground drinking-water sources.

nodes.label: Endangered Species Act (1973)
nodes.definition: Protects threatened and endangered species and the ecosystems they depend on; bars actions that would jeopardize listed species.

nodes.label: RCRA (1976)
nodes.definition: Resource Conservation and Recovery Act — governs the "cradle-to-grave" management and disposal of solid and hazardous waste.

nodes.label: CERCLA / Superfund (1980)
nodes.definition: Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act — funds and compels cleanup of abandoned hazardous-waste sites and holds polluters liable. Prompted by Love Canal.

nodes.label: Wilderness Act (1964)
nodes.definition: Created the National Wilderness Preservation System, protecting designated federal lands in their natural state from development.

nodes.label: TSCA (1976)
nodes.definition: Toxic Substances Control Act — lets the EPA regulate and require safety testing of chemical substances.

nodes.label: Lucas v. South Carolina (1992)
nodes.definition: Landmark Supreme Court case: a regulation depriving an owner of ALL economically beneficial use of land is a regulatory taking requiring just compensation. (David Lucas's beachfront lots.)

nodes.label: Regulatory taking
nodes.definition: When a regulation restricts use of private property so severely it amounts to a "taking" under the Fifth Amendment — requiring the government to pay just compensation.

nodes.label: Fifth Amendment Takings Clause
nodes.definition: Private property shall not "be taken for public use, without just compensation." The constitutional basis for regulatory-taking claims.

nodes.label: NEPA's purpose (exam point)
nodes.definition: To promote informed decision-making by federal agencies by requiring detailed information on the environmental impacts of proposed actions — not to set "less stringent" or state-modifiable policies.

nodes.label: Stockholm Conference (1972)
nodes.definition: The UN Conference on the Human Environment — the first major international environmental meeting. Produced the Stockholm Declaration and led to the creation of UNEP.

nodes.label: Brundtland Report (1987)
nodes.definition: Our Common Future, by the UN World Commission on Environment and Development — defined and popularized sustainable development.

nodes.label: Montreal Protocol (1987)
nodes.definition: International treaty that phased out ozone-depleting substances (CFCs). Widely considered the most successful environmental agreement in history.

nodes.label: Kyoto Protocol (1997)
nodes.definition: First binding treaty committing developed (Annex I) nations to cut greenhouse-gas emissions. The U.S. signed but never ratified it; first commitment period ended 2012.

nodes.label: Copenhagen Accord (2009)
nodes.definition: Non-binding political declaration from COP-15. Recognized the goal of keeping warming below 2°C and pledged $100 billion/yr in climate finance — but set no binding targets. Used "common but differentiated responsibilities."

nodes.label: Paris Agreement (2015)
nodes.definition: Climate accord committing nations to hold warming "well below 2°C" (aiming for 1.5°C) through voluntary Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs).

nodes.label: CITES (1973)
nodes.definition: Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species — regulates and restricts international trade in threatened plants and animals.

nodes.label: Basel Convention (1989)
nodes.definition: International treaty controlling the cross-border movement of hazardous wastes — aimed at stopping dumping from developed onto developing countries.

nodes.label: UNFCCC (1992)
nodes.definition: UN Framework Convention on Climate Change, adopted at the Rio Earth Summit — the parent treaty under which Kyoto, Copenhagen, and Paris were negotiated.

nodes.label: Rio Earth Summit (1992)
nodes.definition: UN Conference on Environment and Development in Rio — produced the UNFCCC, the Convention on Biological Diversity, and Agenda 21.

nodes.label: Common but differentiated responsibilities
nodes.definition: The principle (UNFCCC, Kyoto, Copenhagen) that all nations share responsibility for climate change, but developed countries — having caused most historical emissions — bear a greater obligation.

nodes.label: Precautionary principle
nodes.definition: If an action risks serious or irreversible environmental harm, lack of full scientific certainty should not delay protective measures.

nodes.label: Polluter pays principle
nodes.definition: Those who produce pollution should bear the costs of managing it and remediating the damage.

nodes.label: Greenpeace
nodes.definition: A prominent international non-governmental advocacy organization known for direct-action campaigns against whaling, nuclear testing, and pollution.

nodes.label: World Wildlife Fund (WWF)
nodes.definition: A major international NGO focused on wilderness preservation and reducing human impact on biodiversity.

nodes.label: Nuclear family
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Two parents and their dependent children living together as one household unit.

nodes.label: Extended family
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: A family of three or more generations, or one that includes relatives beyond the parent–child unit, living together or closely tied.

nodes.label: Family of orientation
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: The family you are born into and raised in.

nodes.label: Family of procreation
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: The family you create through marriage/partnership and having or raising children.

nodes.label: Blended family / Stepfamily
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: A family formed when one or both partners bring children from a prior relationship into a new marriage.

nodes.label: Cohabitation
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Two unmarried partners in an intimate relationship living together in the same household.

nodes.label: Fictive kin
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: People treated as relatives despite no blood or marriage tie — e.g., a close friend the children call 'auntie'.

nodes.label: Monogamy
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Marriage to one spouse at a time.

nodes.label: Serial monogamy
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Marrying, divorcing, and remarrying — one spouse at a time, but several across a lifetime.

nodes.label: Polygamy
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Marriage involving more than two spouses at once (the umbrella term).

nodes.label: Polygyny
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: One husband married to two or more wives at the same time.

nodes.label: Polyandry
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: One wife married to two or more husbands at the same time.

nodes.label: Endogamy
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: The norm of marrying WITHIN one's own social group — same race, religion, class, or ethnicity.

nodes.label: Exogamy
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: The norm of marrying OUTSIDE one's own group (e.g., the incest taboo forces marriage outside the immediate family).

nodes.label: Patriarchy
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: A family or social system in which men hold authority and power.

nodes.label: Egalitarian family
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: A family in which partners share power and decision-making roughly equally.

nodes.label: Patrilineal / Matrilineal descent
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Tracing kinship and inheritance through the father's line (patrilineal) or the mother's line (matrilineal).

nodes.label: Structural-functionalism
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Views the family as a system whose parts perform functions that keep society stable — socializing children, regulating sex, providing emotional support. Key: roles exist because they help the system work.

nodes.label: Instrumental vs. expressive roles
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Parsons' claim that families specialize: an instrumental role (breadwinner / task-leader) and an expressive role (nurturer / emotional caretaker).

nodes.label: Manifest vs. latent functions
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Manifest = intended, recognized functions; latent = unintended, hidden consequences.

nodes.label: Conflict theory
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Views the family through power, inequality, and competition for resources. Arrangements persist because they serve the powerful — NOT because they benefit everyone. It critiques hierarchy rather than endorsing it.

nodes.label: Feminist perspective
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Examines how gender inequality is created and reproduced in families — especially women's unequal share of power and unpaid labor.

nodes.label: Symbolic interactionism
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Focuses on the shared meanings, symbols, and definitions people build through everyday interaction, and how these shape roles and the self.

nodes.label: Social construction of reality
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: The idea that people actively create shared meanings — including what counts as 'family' — through interaction.

nodes.label: Social exchange theory
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: People weigh rewards against costs; they stay in a relationship when benefits exceed costs and the alternatives look worse.

nodes.label: Family systems theory
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Treats the family as an interconnected system — a change in one member affects all the others (boundaries, subsystems, feedback loops).

nodes.label: Family development / Life course theory
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Analyzes the predictable stages families pass through over time: marriage, childbearing, launching children, the empty nest.

nodes.label: Ecological theory
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Examines families within nested environments — microsystem, mesosystem, exosystem, and macrosystem.

nodes.label: Postmodern perspective
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Emphasizes family diversity and rejects the idea of one 'normal' or correct family form.

nodes.label: Empirical / scientific method
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Building knowledge from systematic, verifiable observation and evidence rather than opinion or tradition.

nodes.label: Survey
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: A research method that gathers data from many respondents using questionnaires or interviews.

nodes.label: Representative sample
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: A sample that mirrors the larger population, so findings can be generalized to it.

nodes.label: Random sample
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: A sample in which every member of the population has an equal chance of being selected.

nodes.label: Generalizability
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: The extent to which findings from a sample can be applied to the whole population.

nodes.label: Reliability vs. validity
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Reliability = consistent, repeatable results; validity = actually measuring what you intend to measure.

nodes.label: Independent vs. dependent variable
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: The independent variable is manipulated (the presumed cause); the dependent variable is the measured effect.

nodes.label: Correlation vs. causation
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Correlation = two things vary together; causation = one actually produces the other. Correlation alone never proves cause.

nodes.label: Experiment
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: A controlled study that manipulates an independent variable to test its effect, usually using experimental and control groups.

nodes.label: Naturalistic observation
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Watching and recording behavior in its natural setting without manipulating any conditions.

nodes.label: Ethnography / Participant observation
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: In-depth research in which the investigator immerses in and observes a group, sometimes taking part in it.

nodes.label: Informed consent
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Participants must be told a study's purpose, risks, and procedures and must voluntarily agree before taking part.

nodes.label: Confidentiality vs. anonymity
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Confidentiality = the researcher knows identities but won't reveal them; anonymity = even the researcher can't link responses to a person.

nodes.label: Expertise vs. experience
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: Expertise rests on systematic evidence and method; personal experience is real but may not generalize to others.

nodes.label: Socioeconomic status (SES)
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: A person's or family's position based on income, education, and occupation.

nodes.label: Feminization of poverty
nodes.section: Foundations, Forms, Kinship, Theory, Methods, Class
nodes.definition: The trend in which women — especially single mothers — make up a growing share of those living in poverty.

nodes.label: Sex vs. gender
nodes.definition: Sex = biological / physical traits; gender = the social meanings, roles, and expectations attached to being male or female.

nodes.label: Gender role
nodes.definition: The set of behaviors and attitudes a society considers appropriate for each sex.

nodes.label: Gender identity
nodes.definition: A person's internal sense of being male, female, both, or neither.

nodes.label: Gender socialization
nodes.definition: The lifelong process of learning the gender expectations of one's culture, via family, media, peers, and school.

nodes.label: Gender stereotype
nodes.definition: An oversimplified, fixed belief about the traits or abilities of men or women.

nodes.label: Sexual orientation
nodes.definition: A person's enduring romantic / sexual attraction — heterosexual, homosexual, bisexual, and so on.

nodes.label: Cisgender vs. transgender
nodes.definition: Cisgender = gender identity matches birth sex; transgender = gender identity differs from birth sex.

nodes.label: Attachment theory
nodes.definition: Early bonds with caregivers shape how people connect in later relationships, producing secure, anxious, or avoidant patterns.

nodes.label: Secure attachment
nodes.definition: Comfort with both intimacy and independence; trusts others and isn't overly anxious about the relationship.

nodes.label: Anxious / ambivalent attachment
nodes.definition: Craves closeness but fears abandonment; clingy and easily distressed in relationships.

nodes.label: Avoidant attachment
nodes.definition: Uncomfortable with closeness; values independence and keeps emotional distance.

nodes.label: Triangular theory of love
nodes.definition: Love is built from three components: intimacy, passion, and commitment.

nodes.label: Intimacy / Passion / Commitment
nodes.definition: Sternberg's three building blocks: emotional closeness, physical/romantic drive, and the decision to maintain the relationship.

nodes.label: Consummate love
nodes.definition: Sternberg's 'complete' love — intimacy + passion + commitment all present at once.

nodes.label: Companionate love
nodes.definition: Intimacy + commitment without passion; the deep affection of long-married couples whose passion has faded.

nodes.label: Fatuous love
nodes.definition: Passion + commitment without intimacy; a whirlwind courtship that rushes to engagement before partners really know each other.

nodes.label: Romantic love
nodes.definition: Intimacy + passion without commitment.

nodes.label: Infatuation
nodes.definition: Passion alone — without intimacy or commitment; the 'love at first sight' obsession.

nodes.label: Empty love
nodes.definition: Commitment alone — without intimacy or passion.

nodes.label: Styles (colors) of love
nodes.definition: Six love styles: eros (passionate), ludus (game-playing), storge (friendship), pragma (practical), mania (possessive), agape (selfless).

nodes.label: Halo effect
nodes.definition: Assuming physically attractive people also have other desirable traits — smart, kind, successful.

nodes.label: Matching hypothesis
nodes.definition: People tend to pair with partners of roughly similar physical attractiveness.

nodes.label: Sexual script
nodes.definition: Culturally learned guidelines for how, when, and with whom sexual behavior is expected to occur.

nodes.label: Traditional sexual script
nodes.definition: Casts men as the sexual initiators and women as the limit-setters ('gatekeepers'); often paired with a sexual double standard.

nodes.label: Coming out
nodes.definition: The process of recognizing, accepting, and disclosing a gay, lesbian, bisexual, or transgender identity.

nodes.label: Sexual double standard
nodes.definition: Judging the same sexual behavior more harshly for women than for men.

nodes.label: Nonverbal communication
nodes.definition: Conveying meaning without words: facial expression, tone of voice, posture, gesture, eye contact.

nodes.label: Self-disclosure
nodes.definition: Revealing personal information about oneself to a partner; a key way intimacy is built.

nodes.label: Positive vs. negative affect
nodes.definition: Positive affect = warmth, interest, affection, humor; negative affect = hostility, criticism, contempt.

nodes.label: The Four Horsemen
nodes.definition: Gottman's four communication patterns that predict divorce: criticism, contempt, defensiveness, and stonewalling.

nodes.label: Stonewalling
nodes.definition: Withdrawing from interaction, shutting down, and refusing to respond during a conflict.

nodes.label: Contempt
nodes.definition: Treating a partner with disrespect, mockery, or scorn — Gottman's single strongest predictor of divorce.

nodes.label: Demand–withdraw pattern
nodes.definition: One partner pushes to discuss or change something while the other retreats or stonewalls; a destructive cycle.

nodes.label: Principle of least interest
nodes.definition: The partner who cares least about continuing the relationship holds the most power in it.

nodes.label: Homogamy
nodes.definition: Marrying someone with SIMILAR social traits — race, age, religion, education, class.

nodes.label: Heterogamy
nodes.definition: Marrying someone with DISSIMILAR social characteristics.

nodes.label: Hypergamy vs. hypogamy
nodes.definition: Marrying 'up' in social status (hypergamy) or 'down' in social status (hypogamy).

nodes.label: Mating gradient (marriage gradient)
nodes.definition: Women tend to marry men of higher status/age while men marry 'down' — leaving high-status women and low-status men least likely to marry.

nodes.label: Marriage squeeze
nodes.definition: A shortage of marriageable partners for one sex, caused by an imbalance in the sex ratio.

nodes.label: Marriage market
nodes.definition: The metaphor that people 'shop' for partners, trading on assets like looks, income, and status.

nodes.label: Propinquity
nodes.definition: Geographic nearness — people are more likely to meet and marry those who are close by.

nodes.label: Stimulus-value-role theory
nodes.definition: Mate selection moves through three stages: stimulus (initial attraction), value (shared attitudes), role (fit of role expectations).

nodes.label: Pronatalism
nodes.definition: A cultural attitude that everyone should have children and that childbearing is a natural, expected part of adult life.

nodes.label: Child-free vs. childless
nodes.definition: Child-free = chooses NOT to have children; childless = wants children but cannot have them.

nodes.label: Singlehood
nodes.definition: Living as an unmarried adult; can be voluntary or involuntary, temporary or stable.

nodes.label: Widowhood
nodes.definition: The status of having lost a spouse to death — more common for older women, who tend to outlive men.

nodes.label: Authoritative parenting
nodes.definition: High warmth + high control with reasoning: sets firm limits while staying responsive. Linked to the best child outcomes. (The deciding factor vs. permissive is that limits ARE set.)

nodes.label: Authoritarian parenting
nodes.definition: High control, low warmth: demands obedience and uses punishment with little explanation.

nodes.label: Permissive parenting
nodes.definition: Warm and accepting but few rules or demands; children largely regulate themselves.

nodes.label: Uninvolved (neglectful) parenting
nodes.definition: Low warmth and low control; emotionally detached and minimally engaged with the child.

nodes.label: Socialization
nodes.definition: The process by which people learn the norms, values, and skills of their culture.

nodes.label: Culture vs. conduct of fatherhood
nodes.definition: The cultural IDEAL of what a good father is, versus what fathers ACTUALLY do day to day.

nodes.label: Intensive mothering
nodes.definition: A cultural belief that mothering should be child-centered, expert-guided, emotionally absorbing, and labor-intensive.

nodes.label: Concerted cultivation vs. natural growth
nodes.definition: Middle-class parenting that actively cultivates kids through scheduled activities and reasoning, vs. working-class 'accomplishment of natural growth'.

nodes.label: The second shift
nodes.definition: The unpaid housework and child care that employed women perform AFTER their paid workday.

nodes.label: Role conflict
nodes.definition: When the demands of one role (e.g., parent) clash with the demands of another (e.g., employee).

nodes.label: Role strain
nodes.definition: Tension that comes from conflicting demands WITHIN a single role.

nodes.label: Role overload
nodes.definition: Having too many role demands and too little time or energy to meet them all.

nodes.label: Peer marriage
nodes.definition: A marriage in which partners share housework, child care, and decision-making relatively equally.

nodes.label: Sandwich generation
nodes.definition: Adults who are simultaneously caring for their own children and their aging parents.

nodes.label: Sexual harassment
nodes.definition: Unwelcome sexual advances or conduct that creates a hostile or coercive environment, often at work.

nodes.label: Intimate partner violence (IPV)
nodes.definition: Physical, sexual, or psychological harm inflicted by a current or former partner.

nodes.label: Intergenerational transmission of violence
nodes.definition: Children who witness or experience family violence are more likely to use or tolerate it as adults (a social-learning explanation).

nodes.label: Resource theory (of violence)
nodes.definition: Those who lack other resources — income, status, communication skills — may resort to force to get their way.

nodes.label: Exchange / social control theory (of violence)
nodes.definition: Abuse occurs when its rewards outweigh its costs and the abuser expects few negative consequences.

nodes.label: Cycle of violence
nodes.definition: A repeating pattern of tension-building, an acute battering incident, and a calm, loving 'honeymoon' reconciliation phase.

nodes.label: Sibling violence
nodes.definition: Aggression between brothers and sisters — the most common but least reported form of family violence.

nodes.label: Elder abuse
nodes.definition: Physical, emotional, or financial mistreatment, or neglect, of an older adult — often by a caregiver.

nodes.label: Neglect
nodes.definition: Failure to provide a dependent with adequate food, shelter, supervision, medical care, or emotional support.

nodes.label: Emotional / psychological abuse
nodes.definition: Repeated insults, threats, humiliation, or isolation of a family member — without physical contact.

nodes.label: Acquaintance (date) rape
nodes.definition: Sexual assault committed by someone the victim knows, such as a date or partner.

nodes.label: Marital rape
nodes.definition: Forced, nonconsensual sex by a spouse; now a crime in all U.S. states.

nodes.label: No-fault divorce
nodes.definition: Divorce granted without proving wrongdoing by either spouse; spread across the U.S. after 1969–70.

nodes.label: Six stations of divorce
nodes.definition: Bohannan's six dimensions of divorce: emotional, legal, economic, coparental, community, and psychic.

nodes.label: Crude vs. refined divorce rate
nodes.definition: Crude = divorces per 1,000 people; refined = divorces per 1,000 married women (a more accurate measure).

nodes.label: Joint vs. sole custody
nodes.definition: Joint = both parents share legal/physical responsibility; sole = one parent has primary responsibility.

nodes.label: Single-parent family
nodes.definition: A household with one parent living with one or more children.

nodes.label: Binuclear family
nodes.definition: A post-divorce family that spans two households — the mother's and the father's — for the children.

nodes.label: Remarriage
nodes.definition: Marrying again after a divorce or the death of a spouse.

nodes.label: Stepfamily (blended family)
nodes.definition: A family created when a remarriage brings children from a previous relationship.

nodes.label: Incomplete institution
nodes.definition: Cherlin's idea that remarried and stepfamilies lack clear cultural norms and roles to guide them.

nodes.label: Audience analysis
nodes.definition: Gathering and analyzing information about your listeners — their demographics, attitudes, and situation — so you can adapt the speech to their knowledge, interests, and needs. The cornerstone of effective speaking.

nodes.label: Audience-centeredness
nodes.definition: Keeping the audience foremost in mind at every stage. The speaker's goal is to gain a desired response from listeners, not to show off — adapt the message to them.

nodes.label: Demographic analysis
nodes.definition: Audience analysis based on group memberships — age, gender, race/ethnicity, religion, education, socioeconomic status, group affiliations. The most straightforward and verifiable of the three types.

nodes.label: Psychographic analysis
nodes.definition: Audience analysis based on attitudes, beliefs, and values (and opinions). Less verifiable than demographics but essential for persuasion. Values form the foundation that shapes beliefs and attitudes.

nodes.label: Situational analysis
nodes.definition: Audience analysis of the occasion and setting — audience size, physical environment, time, and the reason the audience has gathered.

nodes.label: Three types of audience analysis
nodes.definition: In Stand Up, Speak Out: demographic, psychographic, and situational. Together they tell you who your listeners are, what they think, and the context you'll speak in.

nodes.label: Value
nodes.definition: A deeply held judgment about what is right/wrong, good/bad, desirable/undesirable (e.g., honesty, freedom, fairness). The foundation of psychographic analysis.

nodes.label: Belief
nodes.definition: What a person holds to be true or false. Beliefs rest on values and in turn shape attitudes.

nodes.label: Attitude
nodes.definition: A predisposition to respond favorably or unfavorably toward a person, object, or idea. Knowing whether the audience is favorable, neutral, or hostile shapes your strategy.

nodes.label: Egocentrism
nodes.definition: The tendency of listeners to be concerned above all with their own values, beliefs, and well-being. Speakers must show why the topic matters to them. (Exam term.)

nodes.label: Ethnocentrism
nodes.definition: The belief that one's own group or culture is superior to all others. Speakers must avoid it and adapt respectfully to diverse audiences.

nodes.label: Identification
nodes.definition: Creating common ground with the audience by stressing shared values, experiences, and goals — making listeners feel "this speaker is like me."

nodes.label: Audience adaptation
nodes.definition: Adjusting topic, language, examples, and delivery to fit the specific audience — both while preparing and in real time, by reading audience feedback as you speak.

nodes.label: Audience disposition (key factor)
nodes.definition: What you most need to gauge varies by audience: an audience's existing knowledge of the topic often matters most when informing (e.g., a talk to expectant parents). Match depth and vocabulary to it. (Exam scenario.)

nodes.label: Ethics (in speaking)
nodes.definition: The standards of right and wrong that guide a speaker's conduct. Because speech can influence others, public speaking always carries ethical responsibility.

nodes.label: Speech as power
nodes.definition: Because speechmaking is a form of power, it carries heavy ethical responsibilities. The ability to move audiences must be used honestly and responsibly. (Exam fact.)

nodes.label: Guidelines for ethical speaking
nodes.definition: Make sure your goals are ethically sound; be fully prepared; be honest in what you say; avoid name-calling and abusive language; put ethical principles into practice.

nodes.label: Plagiarism
nodes.definition: Presenting another person's language or ideas as your own. A serious ethical violation. The three types: global, patchwork, and incremental.

nodes.label: Global plagiarism
nodes.definition: Stealing an entire speech from a single source and passing it off as your own. The most blatant and unethical form.

nodes.label: Patchwork plagiarism
nodes.definition: Stitching a speech together by copying from two or three sources without crediting them.

nodes.label: Incremental plagiarism
nodes.definition: Failing to credit specific quotations, paraphrases, or ideas borrowed from others — even when the speech as a whole is your own work. (Exam answer.)

nodes.label: Paraphrasing
nodes.definition: Restating someone's idea in your own words. You must still cite the source when paraphrasing — otherwise it becomes incremental plagiarism.

nodes.label: Oral source citation
nodes.definition: Verbally crediting the origin of facts, quotations, and statistics during the speech ("According to..."). Maintains credibility and prevents plagiarism.

nodes.label: Name-calling / abusive language
nodes.definition: Language that degrades or dehumanizes people or groups. Unethical because it can silence opposing views and inflame prejudice.

nodes.label: Ethical listening
nodes.definition: Listening courteously and attentively, avoiding prejudging the speaker, and supporting the free and open expression of ideas.

nodes.label: NCA Credo for Ethical Communication
nodes.definition: The National Communication Association's statement affirming that ethical, truthful communication is fundamental to responsible decision-making and to a healthy democracy.

nodes.label: Modes of persuasion
nodes.definition: Aristotle's three persuasive appeals: ethos (credibility), pathos (emotion), and logos (logic). Effective persuasion balances all three.

nodes.label: Ethos
nodes.definition: The appeal based on the speaker's credibility — character, competence, and trustworthiness. Built through expertise, honesty, and goodwill toward the audience.

nodes.label: Pathos
nodes.definition: The appeal to the audience's emotions — fear, pity, hope, anger, pride. Powerful, but must be used ethically and balanced with sound reasoning.

nodes.label: Logos
nodes.definition: The appeal based on logic and evidence — facts, statistics, and sound reasoning that support the claim.

nodes.label: Credibility
nodes.definition: The audience's perception of a speaker as competent and trustworthy. Initial (before), derived (during), and terminal (after) credibility. Closely tied to ethos.

nodes.label: Reasoning
nodes.definition: Drawing a conclusion based on evidence — the backbone of logos. Main types: inductive, deductive, causal, analogical, and sign reasoning.

nodes.label: Inductive reasoning
nodes.definition: Reasoning from specific instances to a general conclusion (several examples → a generalization). Conclusions are probable, not certain.

nodes.label: Deductive reasoning
nodes.definition: Reasoning from a general principle to a specific conclusion. Often expressed as a syllogism.

nodes.label: Syllogism
nodes.definition: A three-part deductive argument: major premise, minor premise, conclusion. (All humans are mortal; Socrates is human; therefore Socrates is mortal.)

nodes.label: Causal reasoning
nodes.definition: Reasoning that establishes a cause-and-effect relationship between two events.

nodes.label: Analogical reasoning
nodes.definition: Reasoning by comparison: because two cases are alike in known respects, they are probably alike in a further respect.

nodes.label: Reasoning from sign
nodes.definition: Inferring that something is true from observable indicators (signs) usually associated with it.

nodes.label: Logical fallacy
nodes.definition: An error in reasoning that weakens or invalidates an argument. Spotting and avoiding fallacies is central to critical thinking and ethical persuasion.

nodes.label: Hasty generalization
nodes.definition: Drawing a broad conclusion from insufficient or unrepresentative evidence — too few examples to justify the claim.

nodes.label: False cause (post hoc)
nodes.definition: Assuming that because one event followed another, the first caused the second. Latin: post hoc, ergo propter hoc — "after this, therefore because of this."

nodes.label: Ad hominem
nodes.definition: Attacking the person making an argument rather than the argument itself ("against the man").

nodes.label: Bandwagon (appeal to popularity)
nodes.definition: Assuming something is true or good because many people believe or do it — "everyone's doing it."

nodes.label: Either-or (false dilemma)
nodes.definition: Framing a situation as having only two options when more exist ("You're either with us or against us").

nodes.label: Slippery slope
nodes.definition: Claiming one action will inevitably trigger a chain of extreme consequences, without proving each link in the chain.

nodes.label: Red herring
nodes.definition: Introducing an irrelevant issue to divert attention from the real subject under discussion.

nodes.label: Straw man
nodes.definition: Misrepresenting or exaggerating an opponent's position so it is easier to attack and refute.

nodes.label: Appeal to tradition
nodes.definition: Arguing something should continue simply because "we've always done it that way."

nodes.label: False analogy
nodes.definition: Comparing two things that are not alike in the relevant respects, so the comparison doesn't actually support the claim.

nodes.label: Non sequitur
nodes.definition: "It does not follow." A conclusion that has no logical connection to the evidence or premises offered.

nodes.label: Appeal to pity (ad misericordiam)
nodes.definition: Trying to win support by exploiting the audience's pity or guilt instead of offering sound reasons.

nodes.label: Begging the question
nodes.definition: Circular reasoning — assuming the truth of the conclusion within one of the premises, so the argument proves nothing.

nodes.label: Introduction (functions)
nodes.definition: The opening of a speech. Its jobs: get attention, reveal the topic, establish credibility and goodwill, show relevance to the audience, and preview the main points.

nodes.label: Attention-getter
nodes.definition: The opening device that captures interest: a startling statement, rhetorical question, quotation, story, vivid statistic, or humor.

nodes.label: Preview statement
nodes.definition: A sentence at the end of the introduction that names the main points the speech will cover — a roadmap for listeners.

nodes.label: Body
nodes.definition: The largest part of the speech, containing the main points and supporting material, arranged in a clear organizational pattern.

nodes.label: Main points
nodes.definition: The central claims or key ideas of the speech (usually 2–5), each developed with supporting material.

nodes.label: Conclusion (functions)
nodes.definition: The closing of a speech. Its jobs: signal the end, summarize the main points, and end memorably with a clincher.

nodes.label: Clincher
nodes.definition: A strong, memorable closing line that reinforces the central idea and gives the speech a sense of closure.

nodes.label: Chronological (time) order
nodes.definition: Main points arranged in a time sequence or the steps of a process. Best for historical topics and how-to/demonstration speeches. (Exam: a how-to on making a piñata → time order.)

nodes.label: Spatial order
nodes.definition: Main points arranged by physical location or direction — top to bottom, left to right, east to west.

nodes.label: Topical order
nodes.definition: Dividing the subject into logical subtopics or categories. The most common and flexible pattern.

nodes.label: Causal order
nodes.definition: Main points arranged to show a cause-and-effect relationship (causes → effects, or effects → causes).

nodes.label: Problem-solution order
nodes.definition: First describe a problem, then propose a solution. A staple of persuasive speeches.

nodes.label: Problem-cause-solution order
nodes.definition: A persuasive pattern: identify the problem, analyze its causes, then present a solution.

nodes.label: Comparative advantages order
nodes.definition: A persuasive pattern that compares options and argues that your proposal is superior to the alternatives.

nodes.label: Monroe's Motivated Sequence
nodes.definition: A five-step persuasive pattern designed to spur action: Attention → Need → Satisfaction → Visualization → Action.

nodes.label: Connectives
nodes.definition: Words/phrases that link ideas and keep a speech flowing. Four kinds: transitions, internal previews, internal summaries, and signposts.

nodes.label: Transition
nodes.definition: A word or phrase that signals movement from one idea to the next ("Now that we've seen X, let's turn to Y").

nodes.label: Internal preview
nodes.definition: A statement within the body telling the audience what the speaker will cover next within a point.

nodes.label: Internal summary
nodes.definition: A statement within the body reminding the audience what was just covered before moving on.

nodes.label: Signpost
nodes.definition: A brief cue showing where you are in the speech or stressing importance ("First...", "Most importantly...", "Finally...").

nodes.label: General purpose
nodes.definition: The broad goal of a speech: to inform, to persuade, or to entertain.

nodes.label: Specific purpose
nodes.definition: A single, precise statement of what you want the audience to know, believe, or do — written as an infinitive phrase: "To inform my audience about..."

nodes.label: Central idea (thesis)
nodes.definition: A concise one-sentence statement that captures the essence of the speech and previews its main points.

nodes.label: Informative speech
nodes.definition: A speech that conveys knowledge and understanding — explaining, describing, or demonstrating — without advocating a position.

nodes.label: Persuasive speech
nodes.definition: A speech that aims to influence the audience's beliefs, attitudes, values, or actions.

nodes.label: Narrative speech
nodes.definition: A speech built around a story, used to entertain, illustrate, or make a point through narrative.

nodes.label: Speech to entertain
nodes.definition: A speech whose primary goal is to amuse or delight (e.g., an after-dinner speech), often using humor and storytelling.

nodes.label: Flaws in purpose statements
nodes.definition: Common errors: too broad, contains two unrelated ideas, is figurative, merely expresses opinion, or isn't audience-centered. (Exam: "ban smoking AND increase education spending" = two unrelated ideas.)

nodes.label: Topic selection
nodes.definition: Choosing a subject that is appropriate, suitably narrow, and matched to audience and occasion. "Music" is too broad — decide your purpose first, then narrow the focus. (Exam short-answer.)

nodes.label: Narrowing the topic
nodes.definition: Focusing a broad subject down to a specific, manageable angle that fits the time limit and the audience.

nodes.label: Questions of fact / value / policy
nodes.definition: Persuasive speeches argue whether something is true (fact), good or right (value), or what should be done (policy).

nodes.label: Supporting material
nodes.definition: The evidence used to develop and prove main points: examples, statistics, testimony, facts, and definitions.

nodes.label: Examples
nodes.definition: Specific instances used to illustrate or clarify an idea. They make abstract points concrete and vivid.

nodes.label: Brief example
nodes.definition: A short, specific instance mentioned to illustrate a point; often several are stacked in a row.

nodes.label: Extended example
nodes.definition: A longer, more detailed story or anecdote (a narrative) developed to drive a point home.

nodes.label: Hypothetical example
nodes.definition: An invented but plausible example ("Imagine that...") used to illustrate a situation. Must be identified as hypothetical.

nodes.label: Statistics
nodes.definition: Numerical data showing magnitude, trends, or relationships. Use sparingly, round off, cite the source, and don't read hard-to-remember numbers from memory.

nodes.label: Testimony
nodes.definition: Quotations or paraphrases used to support a point by citing what others have said.

nodes.label: Expert (authoritative) testimony
nodes.definition: Testimony from recognized authorities on the subject. (Exam: quoting a Yale physicist = expert testimony.)

nodes.label: Peer (lay) testimony
nodes.definition: Testimony from ordinary people with firsthand experience — adds a relatable, human dimension.

nodes.label: Direct quotation
nodes.definition: Citing someone's exact words. Must be reproduced accurately and credited to the source.

nodes.label: Facts
nodes.definition: Verifiable statements that can be proven true — the bedrock of credible support.

nodes.label: Definitions
nodes.definition: Explanations of a term's meaning; useful when introducing unfamiliar or technical vocabulary to the audience.

nodes.label: Credentials
nodes.definition: A speaker's qualifications and experience that establish authority to speak on a topic (and build ethos). (Exam: citing veterinary-tech experience = credentials.)

nodes.label: Evaluating evidence
nodes.definition: Strong support is accurate, relevant, current, unbiased, and from a reliable source. Test your evidence before you use it.

nodes.label: Citing sources in support
nodes.definition: Whenever you use a statistic, quotation, or borrowed idea, cite it orally — this builds credibility and avoids incremental plagiarism.

nodes.label: Delivery
nodes.definition: The presentation of a speech through voice and body. Good delivery conveys ideas clearly and engagingly without distracting the audience.

nodes.label: Impromptu delivery
nodes.definition: Speaking with little or no preparation — you organize and think on your feet.

nodes.label: Manuscript delivery
nodes.definition: Reading a speech word-for-word from a full text. Ensures accuracy but risks sounding flat and losing eye contact.

nodes.label: Memorized delivery
nodes.definition: Delivering a speech from memory, word-for-word. Risks sounding mechanical and the danger of forgetting passages.

nodes.label: Extemporaneous delivery
nodes.definition: Speaking from a brief keyword outline after thorough preparation and practice. The most versatile, natural, recommended method — allows real audience contact. (Exam topic.)

nodes.label: Speaking notes (keyword outline)
nodes.definition: Brief notes with key words, delivery cues, and hard-to-remember details (statistics, exact quotations) — not full sentences. Used in extemporaneous speaking.

nodes.label: Vocal delivery (paralanguage)
nodes.definition: How you use your voice: rate, pitch, volume, pauses, vocal variety, articulation, and pronunciation.

nodes.label: Rate
nodes.definition: The speed at which you speak. Vary it for emphasis and avoid rushing.

nodes.label: Pitch
nodes.definition: The highness or lowness of the voice. Varying pitch (inflection) prevents a monotone.

nodes.label: Volume
nodes.definition: The loudness of the voice — loud enough for everyone to hear, and varied for emphasis.

nodes.label: Pause
nodes.definition: A brief, purposeful silence for emphasis or to let an idea land. Avoid vocalized pauses ("um," "uh," "like").

nodes.label: Articulation
nodes.definition: Producing speech sounds clearly and distinctly — avoiding mumbling or slurring.

nodes.label: Pronunciation
nodes.definition: Saying words correctly, with the accepted sounds and stress.

nodes.label: Nonverbal delivery
nodes.definition: Communication through the body: eye contact, facial expressions, gestures, posture, movement, and appearance.

nodes.label: Eye contact
nodes.definition: Looking directly at audience members. The most important nonverbal behavior — builds connection, conveys confidence, and lets you read audience feedback.

nodes.label: Gestures
nodes.definition: Purposeful hand and arm movements that reinforce ideas. Keep them natural, not distracting.

nodes.label: Posture
nodes.definition: How you hold your body. Stand upright and balanced to project confidence and authority.

nodes.label: Nonverbal feedback (from audience)
nodes.definition: Listener cues signaling engagement or boredom: slouching, head-tilting, looking away, fiddling with phones, or turning the body away. Respond by making eye contact, raising energy, and re-engaging. (Exam short-answer.)

nodes.label: Presentation (visual) aids
nodes.definition: Supplementary visual/audio material — slides, objects, charts, graphs, images, video — that clarifies ideas and boosts retention.

nodes.label: Types of visual aids
nodes.definition: Objects/models, photographs, diagrams, charts and graphs (line, bar, pie), maps, video, and the speaker themselves. Pick the type that best clarifies the idea.

nodes.label: Guidelines for visual aids
nodes.definition: Keep them simple and large; display only while discussing them; don't read slides verbatim; practice with them; keep eye contact with the audience, not the screen.

nodes.label: Slide design (recorded speeches)
nodes.definition: Minimal text, readable fonts, high contrast, consistent layout, visuals over walls of words. Slides support you — they're not your script. (Graded by the speech rubric.)

nodes.label: Communication apprehension
nodes.definition: Anxiety about speaking before an audience ("stage fright"). Managed — not eliminated — through preparation, practice, positive visualization, and controlled breathing.
